question: Describe the fundamental difference between a one-way ANOVA and a two-way ANOVA. What key piece of information does a two-way ANOVA provide that a one-way does not?
expectedPoints: One-way ANOVA analyzes the effect of a single categorical independent variable (factor) on a continuous dependent variable.
Two-way ANOVA analyzes the effect of two independent categorical variables (factors) on a continuous dependent variable.
A two-way ANOVA can identify an interaction effect between the two factors, showing if the effect of one factor depends on the level of the other factor, which a one-way ANOVA cannot detect.

question: Explain the purpose of replication in the design of an experiment and describe two specific benefits it provides for the statistical analysis.
expectedPoints: Replication means repeating the experiment or observation multiple times under the same experimental conditions.
It allows for the estimation of experimental error (the natural variability in the data), which is essential for conducting significance tests like ANOVA.
It increases the precision of the estimates of the factor effects and the power of the statistical test to detect significant differences.

question: A computer scientist is testing three new sorting algorithms (A, B, C) and one established algorithm (D). They run each algorithm on four different types of input arrays (Random, Sorted, Reverse Sorted, Nearly Sorted) and measure execution time, with 10 replicates per combination. Describe the full ANOVA model (including all possible effects) that should be used to analyze this data. State the null hypotheses this model would test and explain what a significant interaction effect would mean in this specific context.
expectedPoints: This is a two-factor factorial design. The full ANOVA model would include the main effect for Algorithm (4 levels: A, B, C, D), the main effect for Input Type (4 levels), and the Algorithm*Input Type interaction effect.

The null hypotheses are: H0 for Algorithm - no mean difference in execution time between algorithms; H0 for Input Type - no mean difference in execution time between input types; H0 for Interaction - the effect of algorithm on execution time is independent of the input type (and vice versa).

A significant interaction effect would mean that the performance ranking of the algorithms (which is fastest/slowest) changes depending on the type of input array being sorted. For example, Algorithm A might be fastest on random data but slowest on sorted data.
